critique.summary: Wine 11 introduces significant improvements to running Windows games on Linux, including the new NTSYNC kernel driver that greatly enhances synchronization performance. The release also completes the WoW64 architecture for better 32-bit application support and includes numerous other enhancements such as improved Wayland support and Vulkan updates.
mainPoints: Wine 11 features NTSYNC, a new kernel-level synchronization driver that replaces previous user-space workarounds like esync and fsync, resulting in major performance gains for multi-threaded Windows games on Linux.
NTSYNC has been merged into the mainline Linux kernel (version 6.14), making it widely available without requiring custom kernels or patches.
The WoW64 architecture in Wine 11 is now complete, allowing seamless running of 32-bit Windows applications on 64-bit Linux systems without needing multilib packages.

Additional improvements include enhanced Wayland driver support, Vulkan API version 1.4 support, hardware-accelerated H.264 decoding, better force feedback for gaming peripherals, and numerous bug fixes for popular games.
Wine 11's advancements benefit all projects built on Wine, such as Proton, SteamOS, Lutris, and Bottles, improving the overall Linux gaming experience.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely accurate and supported by verifiable facts. NTSYNC is a real kernel driver merged into Linux kernel 6.14, developed by Elizabeth Figura, and it significantly improves synchronization performance in Wine. The completion of WoW64 in Wine 11 is confirmed by Wine's official release notes and community reports. The performance benchmarks cited align with developer data shared publicly. The improvements to Wayland, Vulkan, and other components are consistent with Wine 11's documented changelog. The article is from XDA Developers, a recognized tech news outlet, and provides detailed technical explanations and references to credible sources such as Linux Plumbers Conference and kernel versioning. However, the article relies heavily on developer benchmarks and statements without extensive third-party independent testing, which is typical for such technical previews.
considerations: Performance improvements may vary depending on specific hardware, Linux distribution, and game titles; not all games will see dramatic gains.
Users need to run Linux kernels version 6.14 or later to benefit from NTSYNC without custom patches.
Some older or less common games might still face compatibility issues despite these improvements.
The article does not provide independent benchmark verification or user experience reports beyond developer data.
The impact on non-gaming Windows applications running through Wine is not extensively discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the recent Wine 11 update, highlighting significant performance improvements in running Windows games on Linux, with some skepticism about the extent of gains compared to existing solutions like fsync. Commenters also touch on the broader implications for Linux gaming, software compatibility, and technical details of the implementation.

comments.author: watashiato
comments.text: Before anyone gets too excited about ntsync, the performance gains are (with few exceptions) mild, usually in the lower single percentage range. These extreme gains are the result of benching against vanilla wine without fsync, anyone playing demanding games on linux would have been doing so using fsync. This is mentioned in the article but treated like a side note. I've been running benchmarks between both and while the performance increase is real, please temper your expectations. A few titles might also run slightly worse.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced and accurate perspective, cautioning that the headline performance gains are mostly relative to vanilla Wine without fsync, which experienced users already employ.

comments.author: hu3
comments.text:
> Dirt 3 went from 110.6 FPS to 860.7 FPS

> Resident Evil 2 jumped from 26 FPS to 77 FPS

> Call of Juarez went from 99.8 FPS to 224.1 FPS

> Tiny Tina's Wonderlands saw gains from 130 FPS to 360 FPS

Amazing. I don't understand the low level details on how such a massive speed gain was ripe for the picking but I welcome!

I guess thanks Valve for pouring money into Proton.
comments.analysis: This comment enthusiastically cites specific benchmark improvements, reflecting the article's claims, though it lacks the context of fsync usage which tempers these numbers.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
Wine is a project that I've grown a near-infinite level of respect for.

I don't know for sure, but I suspect that a lot of the work for Wine is boring and thankless. Digging through and trying to get exact parity with both the documented and undocumented behavior of Windows for the past 30 years doesn't sound fun, but it's finding every little weird edge case that makes Wine a viable product.

The fact that Wine runs a lot of games better than Windows now (especially older games) shows a very strong attention to detail and a high tolerance for pain. I commend them for it.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a respectful and insightful appreciation of the complexity and dedication involved in Wine development, which aligns well with known challenges of the project.

comments.author: evmar
comments.text: If you're interested in technical notes on how the WoW64 thing works, I dug into Wine and implemented a similar thing in my (far inferior) emulator and wrote about it here, including some links to some Wine resources: https://neugierig.org/software/blog/2023/08/x86-x64-aarch64....
comments.analysis: Provides valuable technical context and resources related to Wine's WoW64 implementation, enhancing the discussion with firsthand experience.

comments.author: lifis
comments.text:
It seems like it would be possible to implement this in userspace using shared memory to store the data structures and using just one eventfd per thread to park/unpark (or a futex if not waiting for anything else), which should be fully correct and have similar or faster performance, at the cost of not being secure or robust against process crashes (which isn't a big problem for more Wine usage).

It seems that neither esync or fsync do this though - why?

Claude thinks that "nobody was motivated enough to write and debug the complex shared-memory waiter-list logic when simpler (if less correct) approaches worked for 95% of games, and when correctness finally mattered enough, the kernel was the more natural place to put it". Is that true?
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully explores alternative implementation strategies and references developer insights, contributing a deeper technical perspective.

comments.author: ticulatedspline
comments.text: Wine might be oddly self-defeating. Broad game support on Linux increases the viability of Linux as a desktop, which increases market share, which may result in developers creating Linux ports as a 1st class concern, which don't need Wine to run.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid strategic point about Wine's impact on Linux adoption and native game development, reflecting a common industry observation.

comments.author: mft_
comments.text:
This is great.

Not to sound snarky, but now please get it to run Microsoft Office. I'd argue that this is the last barrier to many, many people being able to use Linux full-time for business purposes.
comments.analysis: Highlights a practical limitation of Wine for business users, emphasizing the importance of Microsoft Office compatibility for wider Linux adoption.

comments.author: freediddy
comments.text: i would love to know how much of these gains are due to help from AI. i have no problem with AI usage at all in coding but i would love to know if the dramatic gains are because of insights from ai usage.
comments.analysis: Speculates about AI involvement in the development gains without evidence; while interesting, this is likely inaccurate given typical Wine development practices.

comments.author: sph
comments.text: I am glad that a portion of the thousands of dollars I've given to Valve Corporation over the years has been gone to improve Wine for everybody. I wonder how many developers and contractors on the project are paid by Valve.
comments.analysis: Expresses gratitude and curiosity about Valve's financial support for Wine development, which is consistent with Valve's known involvement in Proton and Wine.

comments.author: Night_Thastus
comments.text:
I'll be very interested to see how this plays out with final 3rd-party benchmarks.

Now if we can just get some decent Nvidia drivers......
comments.analysis: Shows reasonable anticipation for independent validation of performance claims and highlights ongoing issues with Nvidia drivers on Linux.
